ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn provides a generic pizza class (FOODON_00002682). No specific prosciutto e funghi variant exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pizza as a category. No specific prosciutto and mushroom pizza code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226754000 is the generic pizza concept. No specific topping variant code exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One whole Prosciutto e Funghi Pizza typically weighs approximately 300 grams and constitutes a single-person serving as commonly served in Italian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Pizza, cheese, with ham and mushroom topping — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for pizza with cheese, ham, and mushroom toppings (SR Legacy reference items NDB 21299 and related entries). Per-100g values represent a composite of thin-to-medium crust pizza with tomato sauce, mozzarella, cooked ham, and mushrooms, consistent with a classic Italian prosciutto e funghi style. Values cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods pizza entries. Serving size set at 300 g (whole pizza) consistent with restaurant portion norms.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 derived from University of Sydney GI Database entries for pizza with cheese and tomato topping (GI range 30–60 across studies; mean approximately 53 for regular-crust cheese pizza). Prosciutto e funghi variant is analogous in dough and cheese composition. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (53 × 82.5) / 100 = 43.7, rounded to 43.8. Carbohydrate per serving = 27.5 g/100g × 300 g = 82.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from processed ham (prosciutto) and cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Prosciutto e Funghi Pizza due to its high sodium content of approximately 560 mg per 100 g (1680 mg per whole pizza), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 53) and high glycemic load per serving due to large carbohydrate portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of this pizza is in the low range (53), the glycemic load per whole-pizza serving is high (approximately 43.8), meaning individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to one or two slices and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from mozzarella cheese and processed ham, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Prosciutto e Funghi Pizza in moderation, as the combination of saturated fat (approximately 3.4 g per 100 g) and high sodium (560 mg per 100 g) may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles when consumed in large portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prosciutto e Funghi Pizza is a classic Italian pizza featuring a thin-to-medium wheat-flour dough base topped with tomato sauce, melted mozzarella cheese, sliced prosciutto cotto (Italian cooked ham), and button mushrooms. The pizza is baked in a high-temperature oven until the crust is golden and crisp at the edges while remaining slightly chewy in the centre. The flavour profile combines the savoury, slightly salty character of the prosciutto with the earthy umami of mushrooms and the mild creaminess of melted mozzarella, balanced by the acidity of the tomato base. This pizza is a staple of Italian trattoria menus and is widely served in Italian restaurants globally, including in Singapore where it appears on the menu of establishments such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore. Nutritionally, a 300 g whole pizza provides approximately 660 kcal, 31.5 g protein, 23.4 g fat, and 82.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated due to the processed ham and cheese, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 53 (low range), though the glycemic load per full serving is high, requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish contains gluten, dairy, and pork and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite entries for pizza with cheese, ham, and mushroom toppings. No exact entry for prosciutto e funghi pizza exists in a primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). The USDA adaptation is the highest-quality available approved source for this Western-origin dish. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cheese pizza. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not consistently reported across the USDA FoodData Central pizza entries used as the source basis. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source entries for this pizza category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary USDA source entries for this pizza category. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pizza composite data and should be treated as approximate. The field dataed (Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore - Boat Quay, Singapore) serves this as a menu item at SGD 29.32; price data is preserved from the field survey data source record but is not used as a nutritional evidence source. GI category is low (GI = 53, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving = (53 × 82.5) / 100 = 43.8 g, where 82.5 g is the carbohydrate content of a 300 g serving (27.5 g/100g × 300 g). Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this dish is served in Singapore; Italian is the cuisine of origin and the canonical food name is already in Italian/English. No Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese local names are required by the multilingual matrix for an Italian-cuisine dish served in Singapore context.
